items.title: Hyperparameter sweep with a fixed seed
items.scenario: A team compares learning rates {0.01, 0.001, 0.0001} on the same CNN. They set `random_state=42` for the train/val split and call `torch.manual_seed(42)` before each run. Only learning rate changes — so accuracy differences reflect the hyperparameter, not lucky shuffles.
items.explanation: Fixing the split and initialization isolates the variable under test. Without a shared seed, a better score might come from random batch order, not a better learning rate.

items.title: Re-running a teammate's experiment
items.scenario: Alice logs run `exp-204`: seed 7, commit `a3f9c2`, Docker image `mltrain:2024-06-01`, val accuracy 0.891. Bob checks out the commit, pulls the image, sets the same seeds, and gets 0.889 on GPU. They compare CUDA versions — Alice used cuDNN 8.9, Bob 8.6 — and align environments before declaring a mismatch.
items.explanation: Reproducibility spans seeds *and* pinned stacks. Small environment drift can explain metric gaps even when seeds match.

items.title: Cross-validation with reproducible folds
items.scenario: A sklearn pipeline uses `StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=0)`. Every re-run assigns the same rows to each fold, so mean CV accuracy is comparable across feature-engineering ideas.
items.explanation: CV fold assignment is random unless seeded. Cross-Validation owns fold strategy; this topic owns why `random_state` must be logged.

items.title: Debugging a flaky training job
items.scenario: Overnight training sometimes reaches 92% val accuracy, sometimes 88%. The engineer adds `np.random.seed(0)`, `random.seed(0)`, and `torch.manual_seed(0)` at the top of the script and sets `num_workers=0` temporarily. Runs now match within 0.1% — the flakiness came from unseeded DataLoader shuffling and worker processes.
items.explanation: Multiple RNG streams and multiprocessing are common hidden sources of nondeterminism beyond a single `random_state` argument.
